quiz_questions.question_text: The focal length of a concave mirror is:

options.text: Equal to the radius of curvature

quiz_questions.explanation: For a spherical mirror, the focal length f = R/2, where R is the radius of curvature. The focal point is located halfway between the mirror surface and the center of curvature.

quiz_questions.question_text: The thin lens equation is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/do + 1/di, where f is the focal length, do is the object distance, and di is the image distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A converging (convex) lens has a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A converging (convex) lens has a positive focal length by convention. It bends parallel light rays to converge at the focal point on the opposite side of the lens.

quiz_questions.question_text: The magnification of a mirror or lens is given by:
quiz_questions.explanation: The magnification m = -di/do. A negative m means the image is inverted; a positive m means the image is upright. |m| > 1 means the image is enlarged.

quiz_questions.question_text: A virtual image is one that:

options.text: Can be projected on a screen
Cannot be projected on a screen; light rays only appear to diverge from it
Is always smaller than the object

quiz_questions.explanation: A virtual image is formed where light rays appear to diverge from but do not actually pass through. It cannot be captured on a screen but can be seen by looking through the optical device.

quiz_questions.question_text: When an object is placed between the focal point and a concave mirror, the image is:
quiz_questions.explanation: When an object is between the focal point and a concave mirror, the reflected rays diverge and appear to come from behind the mirror, creating a virtual, upright, and magnified image.

quiz_questions.question_text: A convex mirror always produces images that are:
quiz_questions.explanation: A convex (diverging) mirror always produces virtual, upright, and reduced images, regardless of object distance. This is why convex mirrors are used as wide-angle security mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A diverging (concave) lens has a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A diverging (concave) lens has a negative focal length by convention. It spreads parallel light rays so they appear to diverge from a virtual focal point.

quiz_questions.question_text: When an object is placed at twice the focal length from a converging lens, the image is:

options.text: At 2f on the other side, real, inverted, and same size

quiz_questions.explanation: When do = 2f, by the lens equation: 1/f = 1/(2f) + 1/di, giving di = 2f. The image is real, inverted, and the same size as the object (m = -1).

quiz_questions.question_text: The power of a lens measured in diopters is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The power of a lens in diopters (D) is the reciprocal of the focal length in meters: P = 1/f. A stronger lens has a shorter focal length and higher power.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a concave mirror?

options.text: A curved mirror with a reflecting surface that curves inward (like the inside of a spoon), which converges parallel light rays to a focal point
A mirror that always produces virtual images
A mirror that only reflects colored light

quiz_questions.explanation: A concave (converging) mirror has an inward-curving reflecting surface. It focuses parallel light rays to a real focal point and can produce both real and virtual images depending on object placement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a convex lens?

options.text: A lens that is thinner at the center than at the edges
A lens that is thicker at the center than at the edges, which converges parallel light rays to a focal point
A lens that only produces virtual images

quiz_questions.explanation: A convex (converging) lens is thicker at the center and thinner at the edges. It refracts parallel light rays to converge at the focal point. It is used in magnifying glasses, cameras, and the human eye.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a real image?

options.text: An image that appears to be behind the optical device
An image formed where light rays actually converge and can be projected onto a screen
An image that is always upright
An image seen only through a microscope

quiz_questions.explanation: A real image is formed at a location where light rays actually converge (intersect). It can be captured on a screen and is typically inverted with respect to the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a virtual image?

options.text: An image that exists only in computer simulations
An image formed where light rays appear to diverge from but do not actually pass through; it cannot be projected on a screen
An image that is always inverted
An image formed by concave lenses only

quiz_questions.explanation: A virtual image is formed at a location where light rays, when extended backward, appear to converge. The light does not actually pass through the image location, so it cannot be projected on a screen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the focal point of a lens or mirror?

options.text: The center of the lens or mirror
The point where parallel rays of light converge (or appear to diverge from) after interacting with the lens or mirror
The point where the object is placed

quiz_questions.explanation: The focal point (F) is the point at which rays parallel to the principal axis converge after reflection (mirror) or refraction (lens), or the point from which they appear to diverge for diverging optics.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is magnification in optics?

options.text: The ratio of image height to object height (m = hi/ho = -di/do), indicating how much larger or smaller the image is compared to the object
The focal length of the device
The number of lenses in a system

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnification m = hi/ho = -di/do is the ratio of image size to object size. |m| > 1 means enlarged, |m| < 1 means reduced. The sign indicates orientation (positive = upright, negative = inverted).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the lensmaker's equation?

options.text: An equation relating focal length to image and object distances
An equation that relates a lens's focal length to its index of refraction and the radii of curvature of its surfaces: 1/f = (n-1)(1/R1 - 1/R2)
An equation for calculating lens thickness
An equation for the magnification of a compound lens

quiz_questions.explanation: The lensmaker's equation: 1/f = (n-1)(1/R1 - 1/R2) relates the focal length to the lens material's index of refraction (n) and the radii of curvature of its two surfaces (R1, R2).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the principal axis of a mirror or lens?

options.text: The edge of the mirror or lens
The line passing through the center of the mirror or lens perpendicular to its surface, along which the focal point lies
Any ray of light hitting the mirror
The line connecting the object to the image

quiz_questions.explanation: The principal axis is the imaginary line that passes through the center of the optical element and is perpendicular to its surface. The focal point and center of curvature lie on this axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is spherical aberration?

options.text: The bending of light around obstacles
An optical defect where rays far from the principal axis focus at slightly different points than rays close to the axis, causing a blurred image
The separation of white light into colors
The absorption of light by lens material

quiz_questions.explanation: Spherical aberration occurs because spherical mirrors and lenses focus rays at different distances from the center at slightly different points. It causes blurring and can be corrected with parabolic mirrors or aspheric lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is chromatic aberration?

options.text: Blurring due to the shape of the lens
An optical defect in lenses where different wavelengths of light are focused at slightly different points due to dispersion, causing color fringing
The loss of light intensity through a lens
The darkening at the edges of an image

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatic aberration occurs because the index of refraction varies with wavelength (dispersion). Different colors focus at different distances, creating color fringes around images. It is corrected using achromatic doublets.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object is placed 30 cm from a concave mirror with focal length 10 cm. Where is the image formed?

options.text: 7.5 cm in front of the mirror
15 cm in front of the mirror
30 cm in front of the mirror

quiz_questions.explanation: Using 1/f = 1/do + 1/di: 1/10 = 1/30 + 1/di. 1/di = 1/10 - 1/30 = 3/30 - 1/30 = 2/30. di = 15 cm (in front of the mirror, real image).

quiz_questions.question_text: A converging lens with f = 20 cm forms an image of an object placed 15 cm from the lens. What is the magnification?
A dentist uses a concave mirror with a focal length of 3 cm. The mirror is held 2 cm from a patient's tooth. What is the magnification of the image?
quiz_questions.explanation: 1/3 = 1/2 + 1/di. 1/di = 1/3 - 1/2 = 2/6 - 3/6 = -1/6. di = -6 cm (virtual). m = -(-6)/2 = +3 (upright and magnified, which is what the dentist needs).

quiz_questions.question_text: An optometrist prescribes a lens with power -2.5 diopters. What is the focal length and type of this lens?
quiz_questions.explanation: f = 1/P = 1/(-2.5) = -0.4 m = -40 cm. The negative focal length indicates a diverging (concave) lens, used to correct myopia (nearsightedness).

quiz_questions.question_text: A convex security mirror in a store has a focal length of -60 cm. A shoplifter stands 3 m away. Where does the image appear and what is the magnification?

options.text: di = +50 cm in front of mirror, m = -0.167
di = +30 cm in front of mirror, m = -0.1

quiz_questions.question_text: A projector uses a converging lens with f = 10 cm. A slide is placed 10.5 cm from the lens. How far away should the screen be placed?
A telescope objective lens has f = 100 cm and the eyepiece has f = 5 cm. What is the angular magnification of the telescope?
quiz_questions.explanation: Angular magnification of a telescope: M = -f_objective/f_eyepiece = -100/5 = -20. The magnitude is 20x (the negative sign indicates an inverted image).

quiz_questions.question_text: Two thin lenses with focal lengths f1 = 20 cm and f2 = -30 cm are placed in contact. What is the effective focal length of the combination?
quiz_questions.explanation: For thin lenses in contact: 1/f = 1/f1 + 1/f2 = 1/20 + 1/(-30) = 3/60 - 2/60 = 1/60. So f = 60 cm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A compound microscope has an objective lens (f = 0.5 cm) and an eyepiece (f = 2.5 cm). The tube length is 16 cm. What is the total magnification when the final image is at the near point (25 cm)?
A photographer uses a camera with a 50 mm lens to photograph a person 5 m away. What is the image distance (distance from lens to film/sensor)?
quiz_questions.explanation: 1/50 = 1/5000 + 1/di (all in mm). 1/di = 1/50 - 1/5000 = 100/5000 - 1/5000 = 99/5000. di = 5000/99 = 50.5 mm. The image forms very close to the focal length.
